document_text:
Historical Consensus:
Preventing Posterior Collapse via
Iterative Selection of Gaussian Mixture Priors
Zegu Zhang ( zeguzhang@outlook.com)
Jian Zhang (tsegoochang2000@gmail.com)
March 12, 2026
Abstract
Variational autoencoders (VAEs) frequently suffer from posterior collapse, where latent variables
become uninformative and the approximate posterior degenerates to the prior. Recent work has
characterized this phenomenon as a phase transition governed by the spectral properties of the data
covariance matrix. In this paper, we propose a fundamentally different approach: instead of avoiding
collapse through architectural constraints or hyperparameter tuning, weeliminate the possibility of
collapse altogetherby leveraging the multiplicity of Gaussian mixture model (GMM) clusterings.
We introduceHistorical Consensus Training, an iterative selection procedure that progressively
refines a set of candidate GMM priors through alternating optimization and selection. The key
insight is that models trained to satisfy multiple distinct clustering constraints develop ahistorical
barrier—a region in parameter space that remains stable even when subsequently trained with a
single objective. We prove that this barrier excludes the collapsed solution, and demonstrate through
extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets that our method achieves non-collapsed
representations regardless of decoder variance or regularization strength. Our approach requires no
explicit stability conditions (e.g.,σ′2< λ max) and works with arbitrary neural architectures. The
code is available athttps://github.com/tsegoochang/historical-consensus-vae.
1 Introduction
Variational autoencoders (VAEs) [Kingma and Welling, 2013] are a cornerstone of deep generative
modeling, combining variational inference with neural networks to learn latent representations of high-
dimensional data. Despite their widespread success, VAEs are plagued byposterior collapse[Bowman
et al., 2015, Lucas et al., 2019], a phenomenon where the approximate posteriorq ϕ(z|x) becomes indis-
tinguishable from the priorp(z), rendering the latent variables uninformative.
Recent theoretical advances have revealed that posterior collapse is not merely an optimization artifact
but aphase transitiongoverned by the interplay between data structure and model hyperparameters
[Li et al., 2024]. For deep Gaussian VAEs, collapse occurs when the decoder varianceσ′2exceeds the
largest eigenvalueλ maxof the data covariance matrix. This insight has led to practical guidelines: to
avoid collapse, one must ensureσ′2< λmax.
However, this condition is inherentlyrestrictive—it imposes a hard constraint on model architecture
and hyperparameters. Moreover, it addresses collapse byavoidingthe unstable region rather than
eliminatingthe possibility of collapse itself.
In parallel, a separate line of research has explored combining VAEs with Gaussian mixture models
(GMMs) to enhance representational power [Liu et al., 2023, Dilokthanakul et al., 2016]. These models
treat the latent space as a mixture of Gaussians, enabling clustering and generation within a unified
framework. However, they do not directly address the posterior collapse problem.
Our key observationis that GMM clustering of the same dataset yieldsmultiple distinct solu-
tionsdue to random initialization and the non-convexity of the EM algorithm. These solutions represent
different but equally valid ways to partition the data. We argue that this multiplicity, often viewed as a
nuisance, is actually avaluable resourcefor preventing posterior collapse.
Main idea.We propose to train a VAE byiteratively selecting among multiple GMM clus-
tering results. Starting with a diverse set of candidate priors, we alternate between:
•Training the VAE to satisfy all current clustering constraints (via a conditioned loss),
1arXiv:2603.10935v1  [cs.LG]  11 Mar 2026

•Evaluating the model’s performance on each constraint,
•Retaining only the best-performing half of the candidates.
This process continues until only two candidates remain, followed by refinement training to an extremely
low loss threshold (<10−5), and finally training with a single candidate.
Why it works.Models trained through this procedure develop what we call ahistorical barrier:
the parameter trajectory is constrained by the requirement to satisfy all previously selected clustering
constraints. The collapsed solution, which would incur high loss on these historical constraints, lies out-
side the feasible region. Remarkably,even when finally trained with a single clustering objective,
the model remains non-collapsed, held in place by the memory of its training history.
Contributions.
1. We introduceHistorical Consensus Training, a novel framework that prevents posterior collapse
by leveraging the multiplicity of GMM clusterings.
2. We prove the existence of ahistorical barrierthat separates non-collapsed solutions from col-
lapsed ones, and show that models trained with our method reside in the non-collapsed region.
3. We demonstrate empirically that our method achieves non-collapsed representationswithout any
explicit stability conditions(e.g.,σ′2< λmax), across multiple datasets and architectures.
4. We provide theoretical and experimental evidence that even when reduced to a single clustering
objective, models retain their non-collapsed state due to historical inertia.
2 Related Work
2.1 Posterior Collapse in VAEs
Posterior collapse has been extensively studied since the introduction of VAEs [Bowman et al., 2015].
Early explanations focused on the KL divergence term in the ELBO, which encourages the posterior to
match the prior [Higgins et al., 2017] . This led to heuristic solutions such as KL annealing [Huang et al.,
2018] andβ-VAE [Higgins et al., 2017].
More recent work has provided rigorous theoretical analyses. Lucas et al. [2019] studied linear VAEs
and identified the role of the log marginal likelihood. Dai et al. [2020] examined local optima. Crucially,
Li et al. [2024] characterized posterior collapse as a phase transition, deriving the conditionσ′2> λmax
for collapse onset. Ichikawa and Hukushima [2025] extended this analysis using the replica method,
revealing dataset-size dependence.
Despite these advances, all existing approaches treat collapse as something to beavoidedthrough
careful design. Our work is the first toeliminatecollapse by leveraging solution multiplicity.
2.2 Gaussian Mixture VAEs
Combining VAEs with mixture priors has a rich history. The GMVAE [Dilokthanakul et al., 2016]
introduces a categorical variable to model cluster assignments. Liu et al. [2023] proposed Cloud-VAE
with concept embeddings. Liu et al. [2024] introduced dimension-weighting for disentanglement. These
works focus on enhancing latent representations, not on preventing collapse.
Our work differs in that we usemultiple GMM clusterings of the same dataastraining
constraints, rather than as a fixed prior.
2.3 Multi-Task and Continual Learning
Our iterative selection procedure bears resemblance to multi-task learning [Caruana, 1997] and cur-
riculum learning [Bengio et al., 2009]. The key difference is that our tasks (clustering constraints) are
generated from the same data, ensuring compatibility. The concept ofhistorical barrieris related to
elastic weight consolidation[Kirkpatrick et al., 2017] in continual learning, where past tasks constrain
future optimization.
2

3 Preliminaries
3.1 Variational Autoencoders
A VAE models datax∈RNusing latent variablesz∈Rnwith priorp(z) =N(0, I). The encoder
qϕ(z|x) approximates the true posterior, and the decoderp θ(x|z) generates reconstructions. Training
maximizes the evidence lower bound (ELBO):
ELBO(x) =E qϕ(z|x)[logp θ(x|z)]−KL(q ϕ(z|x)∥p(z)).(1)
For Gaussian VAEs, we have:
pθ(x|z) =N(x;f θ(z), σ′2I),(2)
qϕ(z|x) =N(z;µ ϕ(x),diag(σ2
ϕ(x))).(3)
3.2 Posterior Collapse as Phase Transition
Li et al. [2024] showed that posterior collapse corresponds to the stability of the trivial solutionq ϕ(z|x) =
p(z),p θ(x|z) =p data(x). By analyzing perturbations around this solution, they derived the collapse
condition:
σ′2> λmax,(4)
whereλ maxis the largest eigenvalue of the data covariance matrix Σ =E pdata[(x− ⟨x⟩)(x− ⟨x⟩)T].
3.3 Gaussian Mixture Models
A GMM withKcomponents represents a distribution as:
p(x) =KX

withP
kπk= 1,π k≥0. Parameters are typically estimated via the EM algorithm, which is sensitive to
initialization and yields multiple local optima.

Given a GMM clustering resultC={π k, µk,Σk}K
k=1, we can define a conditioned loss that encourages

4 Method: Historical Consensus Training
4.1 Motivation: Multiplicity as a Resource
Given a datasetD={x i}M
i=1, running EM with different initializations yields a set of clustering re-
sults{C 1,C2, . . . ,C R}. These results differ in their component parameters but all achieve comparable
likelihood.We treat this multiplicity as a feature, not a bug.
If we train a VAE to satisfy multiple distinct clustering constraints simultaneously, the model must
develop a representation flexible enough to accommodate all of them. This flexibility inherently precludes
posterior collapse, as the collapsed solution cannot satisfy diverse constraints.
4.2 The Selection Pipeline
Our method proceeds in three stages, illustrated in Figure 1.
3

Figure 1: Overview of Historical Consensus Training. (1) Run EM multiple times to obtain diverse
clusterings. (2) Iteratively train the VAE with all current clusterings and retain the best half. (3) Refine
with the final two clusterings to ultra-low loss. (4) Train with a single clustering to verify non-collapse.
4.2.1 Stage 1: Power-of-Two Selection
LetR 0= 2kbe the initial number of clustering results. Fort= 0,1, . . . , T−1:
1. Train the VAE forEepochs, cycling through all clustering results inR t. For each epoch, we
minimize:
Ltotal=L VAE+β· L C(x,ˆx),(7)
whereL Cis the clustering consistency loss.
2. After training, evaluate the model’s performance on each clustering resultC ∈R t:
ℓC=1
|D|X
x∈Dmin
k∥ˆx(x)−µC
k∥2.(8)
3. Retain the|R t|/2 clustering results with the smallestℓ C. SetR t+1to this subset.
Continue until|R t|= 2.
4.2.2 Stage 2: Consensus Refinement
With the final two clustering resultsC a,Cb, continue training until:
max(ℓ Ca, ℓCb)< ϵ,(9)
whereϵis a very small threshold (e.g., 10−5). This ensures the model satisfies both constraints to high
precision.
4.2.3 Stage 3: Final Single-Cluster Training
As a stress test, we continue training with onlyC a(orC b) for an additionalE finalepochs. We monitor
the KL divergence to detect any signs of collapse.
4.3 Theoretical Analysis: The Historical Barrier
Definition 4.1(Historical Loss).For a model with parameters Θ, the historical loss with respect to a
set of clustering resultsSis:
LS(Θ) = max
C∈SℓC(Θ).(10)
Definition 4.2(Feasible Region).The feasible region after stagetis:
Ft={Θ :L St(Θ)≤ϵ t},(11)
whereS tis the set of retained clusterings andϵ tis the maximum loss achieved during training.
4

Lemma 4.3(Nested Feasible Regions).The feasible regions are nested:F T⊂ FT−1⊂ ··· ⊂ F 0.
Proof.Each stage retains only clusterings with the smallest losses, and training ensuresL St(Θt)≤ϵ t.
SinceS t+1⊂St, any Θ satisfyingL St+1(Θ)≤ϵ t+1also satisfiesL St(Θ)≤max(ϵ t, ϵt+1), but the training
process ensuresϵ t+1≤ϵt, henceF t+1⊂ Ft.
Theorem 4.4(Exclusion of Collapsed Solutions).LetΘ collapse be any collapsed solution (i.e.,q ϕ(z|x) =
p(z)). Then there exists a constantδ >0such that for any clustering resultC,ℓ C(Θcollapse )≥δ. If
ϵT< δ, thenΘ collapse /∈ FT.
Proof.At a collapsed solution, reconstructions are independent ofzand equal toE pdata[x] (for optimal
decoder) or some constant. The distance to any non-trivial cluster meanµC
kis bounded below by the
minimum separation between cluster means and the data mean, which is positive for any reasonable
clustering. Takingδas the minimum over allC ∈S 0of this distance gives the result.
Corollary 4.5(Historical Inertia).IfΘ∗∈ FT, then even when trained subsequently with a single cluster-
ingC/∈S T, gradient descent cannot reachΘ collapse without passing through regions whereL ST(Θ)> ϵ T.
This corollary explains our empirical finding: models refined on multiple constraints retain a “mem-
ory” that prevents collapse even when the constraints are removed.
4.4 Why Power-of-Two?
The choice ofR 0= 2kand halving each round is deliberate:
•It creates a clear hierarchy of constraints, from diverse to focused.
•The exponential decay ensures rapid convergence in number of rounds (log2R0rounds total).
•It guarantees we end with exactly 2 clusterings, enabling pairwise refinement.
5 Experiments
5.1 Experimental Setup
5.1.1 Datasets
We evaluate on:
5

•Synthetic GMM: 50,000 samples from an 8-component GMM in 32 dimensions, with component
variances controlled to be below a threshold.
•MNIST: 60,000 grayscale images, resized to 14×14 (196 dimensions).
•Fashion-MNIST: 70,000 grayscale images, resized to 14×14.
•CIFAR-10: 60,000 color images, converted to grayscale and resized to 8×8 (64 dimensions)
following Li et al. [2024].
5.1.2 Baselines
We compare against:
•Vanilla VAE [Kingma and Welling, 2013]
•β-VAE [Higgins et al., 2017] withβ∈ {1,2,4,8}
•KL Annealing [Huang et al., 2018]
•EM-type VAE [Anonymous, 2023]
5.1.3 Architecture
For fair comparison, all methods use the same encoder/decoder architecture per dataset:
•Synthetic/MNIST/Fashion: MLP with hidden layers [256,128] for encoder, [128,256] for decoder.
•CIFAR-10: Convolutional encoder (two Conv+ReLU layers) and transposed convolutional decoder.
Latent dimensionn= 8 for all experiments.
5.1.4 Evaluation Metrics
•KL divergence: AverageD KL(q(z|x)∥p(z)) over test set. Values near zero indicate collapse.
•Active units: Number of latent dimensions withE[σ2
j(x)]<0.99 [Burda et al., 2015].
•Reconstruction error: Mean squared error between input and reconstruction.
•FID(for CIFAR-10): Frechet Inception Distance [Heusel et al., 2017].
5.2 Quantitative Results
We evaluate our method on three datasets under the violating conditionσ′2= 2λ max. Table 1 shows that
our method achievesD KL>2.0 on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, while vanilla VAE collapses completely
(DKL<0.01).
Active Units Analysis.Despite the high KL values, we observe that the number of active latent
units remains limited. On MNIST, only 2 out of 48 dimensions are active (variance>0.005), with the
remaining dimensions having near-zero variance (∼10−5). Similarly, Fashion-MNIST has 3 active units
and CIFAR-10 has 3 active units.
This indicates that while our method successfully prevents complete collapse, the model tends to
concentrate information into a small subset of dimensions. Improving the distribution of information
across latent dimensions remains an important direction for future work.
5.3 Verification of Historical Barrier
We first verify that models trained with our method indeed retain memory of discarded clusterings.
Figure 2 (left) shows the loss on discarded clusterings (those discarded in early rounds) over the course
of training. Despite never being trained on these after round 1, the loss remains low, confirming historical
memory.
We also explicitly construct a collapsed solution by training a VAE with extremely high decoder
variance (σ′2= 10λ max). Figure 2 (right) shows the Euclidean distance in parameter space between our
model and this collapsed solution, which increases over time, confirming separation.
6

Figure 2: Verification of historical barrier. Left: Loss on discarded clusterings remains low throughout
training, indicating memory. Right: Distance to collapsed solution increases as training progresses.

5.4 Posterior Collapse Prevention
Table 1 compares our method against baselines. Key observations:
•Our method achieves the highest KL divergence (indicating least collapse) across all datasets, with
values ranging from 2.49 to 3.70, far above the collapse threshold of 0.01.
•On Synthetic, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10, the final single-cluster training maintains high KL,
confirming historical inertia.
•On MNIST, the final stage occasionally collapses due to aggressive regularization (β= 4.0), but
the post-refinement result (D KL= 2.51) already demonstrates successful prevention of posterior
collapse.
•Active units remain limited (2-5 out of 48), indicating that while collapse is prevented, the repre-
sentation is not fully distributed.
Figure 3 shows the KL divergence trajectories during training. Our method maintains consistently
high KL across all datasets, confirming that posterior collapse is successfully prevented.
5.5 Ablation Studies
5.5.1 Initial Number of Clusterings
Figure 4 (left) shows final KL divergence as a function ofR 0= 2k. Performance improves withR 0up
toR 0= 16, then saturates. We useR 0= 16 for all experiments.
7

Figure 3: KL divergence trajectories across datasets. Our method maintains high KL throughout train-
ing, demonstrating effective prevention of posterior collapse.
Table 2: Results on CIFAR-10 with convolutional architecture.
Method KL↓Active↑MS-SSIM↑

β-VAE (β= 2) 0.25 3.1 0.38
KL Annealing 0.31 4.2 0.42

5.5.2 Refinement Thresholdϵ
Figure 4 (right) shows the effect ofϵon final KL. Extremely smallϵ(10−5) yields best results, but even
ϵ= 10−3outperforms baselines.
5.5.3 Selection Ratio
We experimented with keeping fractions other than 1/2 (e.g., 1/3, 2/3). The 1/2 ratio consistently
performed best, balancing diversity and focus.
5.6 Architecture Independence
To demonstrate that our method does not rely on specific architectures, we repeated experiments on
CIFAR-10 with convolutional encoder/decoder. Table 2 shows results; our method again achieves non-
collapsed representations.
5.7 Comparison with Stability Condition
Recall that Li et al. [2024] derived the conditionσ′2< λmaxto avoid collapse. We test our method under
violating conditions: settingσ′2= 2λ maxandσ′2= 5λ max. Figure 5 shows that our method maintains
8

thresholdϵ.
Figure 5: KL divergence under violating conditions (σ′2> λ max). Our method remains non-collapsed
while vanilla VAE collapses.
non-zero KL even when the condition is violated, while vanilla VAE collapses completely.
6 Discussion
6.1 The Nature of Historical Memory
Our results suggest that the model’s parameters encode information about all previously seen clustering
constraints, even after those constraints are removed. This is reminiscent ofelastic weight consolida-
tion[Kirkpatrick et al., 2017] in continual learning, where important weights are protected from large
changes. In our case, the ”protection” emerges naturally from the training dynamics rather than explicit
regularization.
6.2 Limitations
While powerful, our method has limitations:
•Computational cost: Running EMR 0times and multiple training cycles increases total compu-
tation. However, EM runs are parallelizable, and the total training time remains acceptable (2-3x
vanilla VAE).
9

•Choice ofR 0andϵ: Optimal values may be dataset-dependent, though our experiments show
robustness.
•Clustering quality: If all EM runs produce very similar clusterings, diversity is low and the
method reduces to standard training. This can be detected by measuring pairwise distances between
clustering results.
•Limited active units: While collapse is prevented, the representation is not fully distributed
(only 2-5 active units out of 48), suggesting room for improvement in representation efficiency.
6.3 Broader Implications
Our work suggests a new paradigm for preventing undesirable solutions in deep learning: instead of
designing constraints to avoid them, we can leverage solution multiplicity totrain them out of exis-
tence. This principle may extend beyond VAEs to other models with multiple valid solutions, such as
GANs or energy-based models.
7 Implications for Diffusion Models
Our analysis of posterior collapse as a phase transition and the proposed historical consensus training
have significant implications for diffusion models, which have emerged as powerful alternatives to VAEs
for generative modeling.
7.1 The Posterior Collapse Analogy in Diffusion Models
Diffusion models [Ho et al., 2020, Song et al., 2020] learn to reverse a gradual noising process. While they
do not suffer from posterior collapse in the VAE sense, they face an analogous problem:information
loss in the reverse process.
In a VAE, posterior collapse occurs when the encoderq ϕ(z|x) becomes independent ofx. In a
diffusion model, the reverse processp θ(xt−1|xt) could similarly become independent ofx tif the model
is too expressive or poorly conditioned. This manifests as:
•Mode dropping: The model fails to capture certain data modes
•Unconditional generation degrading: The model ignores the conditioning onx t
•Deterministic reverse: The process becomes nearly deterministic, losing stochasticity
7.2 The Spectral Condition Applies to Diffusion
Remarkably, the spectral conditionσ′2> λ maxthat governs posterior collapse in VAEs has a direct
analogue in diffusion models.
Consider the forward diffusion process:
q(xt|x0) =N(x t;√¯αtx0,(1−¯α t)I) (12)
The variance 1−¯α tincreases witht. When this variance exceeds the data covariance’s maximum
eigenvalueλ max, the signalx 0becomes indistinguishable from noise [Choi et al., 2022]. This defines a
critical timestept c:
1−¯α tc=λmax (13)
Fort > t c, the reverse process must rely purely on learned priors rather than the observedx t,
analogous to posterior collapse.
7.3 Historical Consensus for Diffusion Training
Our historical consensus training can be adapted to diffusion models. The key insight: just as we
used multiple GMM clusterings to create diverse constraints, we can usemultiple noise schedulesor
multiple diffusion trajectoriesas constraints.
This trains the model to handle diverse noise schedules, creating a historical barrier that prevents it
from collapsing to schedule-independent behavior.
10

Algorithm 2Historical Consensus for Diffusion Models
Require:DatasetD, number of noise schedulesR= 2k
Ensure:Trained diffusion modelϵ θ
GenerateRdifferent noise schedules{β(1)

Compute lossL=∥ϵ−ϵ θ(xt, t, β)∥2
Updateθ
end for

Keep only the|S|/2 schedules with smallest loss
end while

7.4 Empirical Predictions
Based on our VAE analysis, we make the following predictions for diffusion models:
1.Critical timestep: There exists a timestept csuch that fort > t c, the reverse process becomes
independent ofx t, analogous to posterior collapse.
2.Spectral condition:t cis determined byλ maxof the data covariance.
3.Historical barrier: Training with multiple noise schedules prevents schedule-independent col-
lapse, improving sample diversity.
4.Inference flexibility: Models trained with historical consensus can be sampled with different
schedules at test time without degradation.
7.5 Preliminary Evidence
Recent work provides indirect support for these predictions. Choi et al. [2022] showed that diffusion
models have a critical timestep beyond which the signal is lost. Karras et al. [2022] demonstrated
that training with multiple noise schedules improves performance. Our framework provides a unified
theoretical explanation for these observations.
7.6 Future Directions
This connection opens several research directions:
•Adaptive scheduling: Dynamically adjust noise schedules based on data covariance to avoid the
critical region.
•Historical sampling: Use multiple schedules during inference to improve sample diversity.
•Theoretical unification: Develop a unified phase transition theory for both VAEs and diffusion
models.
•Architecture design: Design networks that maintain sensitivity tox teven at high noise levels.
The phase transition perspective we developed for VAEs thus provides a powerful lens for understand-
ing and improving diffusion models, suggesting that posterior collapse is not a VAE-specific pathology
but a fundamental phenomenon in generative modeling.

Figure 6: Illustration of the critical timestep in diffusion models. When the noise variance exceedsλ max,
the signal becomes indistinguishable from noise, analogous to posterior collapse in VAEs.
8 Conclusion
We have introduced Historical Consensus Training, a novel method that prevents posterior collapse in
VAEs by iteratively selecting among multiple GMM clustering results. Our key insight is that models
trained to satisfy diverse constraints develop a historical barrier that excludes collapsed solutions, even
when subsequently trained with a single objective. We provided theoretical analysis of this barrier and
empirical validation across multiple datasets and architectures.
Our method achievesD KL>2.5 on synthetic data,>2.0 on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, and
>3.5 on CIFAR-10 under the violating conditionσ′2= 2λ max, where vanilla VAE collapses completely
(DKL<0.01). This demonstrates that posterior collapse is not inevitable and can be prevented through
historical consensus training.
However, we observe that the number of active latent units remains limited (2-5 out of 48), suggesting
that while collapse is prevented, the representation is not fully distributed. Future work will focus on
encouraging more balanced use of latent dimensions through improved regularization and architectural
innovations.
Acknowledgments
This work was supported by the authors’ institutions. The authors thank the anonymous reviewers for
their valuable feedback.

B Experimental Details
B.1 Hyperparameters
Table 3: Hyperparameters used in experiments.
Parameter Synthetic/MNIST/Fashion CIFAR-10
Latent dimensionn48 48
Encoder hidden dims [1024,512,256] Conv layers: 32,64,128

Batch size 512 512

B.2 Computational Resources
Experiments were conducted on a single NVIDIA RTX 4080 SUPER GPU. Total runtime for our method
(including EM runs) is approximately 4-6 hours per dataset, compared to 1-2 hours for vanilla VAE.
C Additional Results

column a round. Dark cells indicate retention.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a novel approach to preventing posterior collapse in variational autoencoders (VAEs) by leveraging the multiplicity of Gaussian mixture model (GMM) clusterings. The main points covered include the introduction of Historical Consensus Training, a framework that iteratively selects among multiple GMM clustering results to refine a set of candidate priors, and the concept of a "historical barrier" that separates non-collapsed solutions from collapsed ones. The document also discusses related work on posterior collapse in VAEs, Gaussian mixture VAEs, and multi-task and continual learning, highlighting the differences between the proposed approach and existing methods. The key contributions of the work include the introduction of Historical Consensus Training, the proof of the existence of a historical barrier, and empirical demonstrations of the method's effectiveness in achieving non-collapsed representations without explicit stability conditions.

structure.title: Related Work
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a novel framework called Historical Consensus Training that prevents posterior collapse in Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) by leveraging multiple Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) clusterings. The main points covered include the introduction of the Historical Consensus Training framework, the concept of an "ahistorical barrier" that separates non-collapsed solutions from collapsed ones, and empirical demonstrations of the method's effectiveness across multiple datasets and architectures. The document also reviews related work on posterior collapse in VAEs, Gaussian Mixture VAEs, and multi-task and continual learning, highlighting the unique approach of using multiple GMM clusterings as training constraints to prevent collapse.

structure.title: Preliminaries
structure.summary: The partial document discusses variational autoencoders (VAEs), posterior collapse, and Gaussian mixture models (GMMs). It introduces the concept of VAEs, their components, and the evidence lower bound (ELBO) used for training. The document also explains posterior collapse as a phase transition and its relation to the stability of the trivial solution. Additionally, it covers GMMs, their representation, and parameter estimation via the EM algorithm. The partial document then proposes a method called Historical Consensus Training, which utilizes the multiplicity of clustering results from different initializations as a resource to prevent posterior collapse in VAEs. The method involves training a VAE to satisfy multiple distinct clustering constraints simultaneously, promoting flexibility in the model's representation and preventing posterior collapse.

nodes.title: Variational Autoencoders
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses variational autoencoders (VAEs), posterior collapse, and Gaussian mixture models (GMMs). It covers the basics of VAEs, including the evidence lower bound (ELBO) and the role of the encoder and decoder. The document also explores posterior collapse as a phase transition and its relation to the stability of the trivial solution. Additionally, it introduces GMMs and their application in conditioning VAE training. The main points also touch on historical consensus training, which leverages the multiplicity of clustering results to develop a flexible representation that precludes posterior collapse. The method involves a selection pipeline with three stages to achieve this goal.

nodes.title: Posterior Collapse as Phase Transition
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses variational autoencoders (VAEs), posterior collapse, and Gaussian mixture models (GMMs). It introduces the concept of VAEs, their components, and the evidence lower bound (ELBO) used for training. The document also explains posterior collapse as a phase transition and the collapse condition. Additionally, it covers GMMs, their representation, and parameter estimation via the EM algorithm. The partial document then proposes a method called Historical Consensus Training, which utilizes the multiplicity of clustering results from the EM algorithm as a resource to prevent posterior collapse in VAEs. The method involves training a VAE to satisfy multiple distinct clustering constraints simultaneously, promoting flexibility in the model's representation and preventing posterior collapse.

nodes.title: Gaussian Mixture Models
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses variational autoencoders (VAEs), posterior collapse, and Gaussian mixture models (GMMs). nodes.title: GMM-Conditioned VAE Training
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses variational autoencoders (VAEs) and their training methods. The main points covered include the basics of VAEs, posterior collapse as a phase transition, Gaussian mixture models (GMMs), and GMM-conditioned VAE training. The document also introduces a new method called Historical Consensus Training, which involves training a VAE to satisfy multiple distinct clustering constraints simultaneously to prevent posterior collapse. The method consists of three stages: power-of-two selection, consensus refinement, and final single-cluster training. Theoretical analysis of the historical loss and feasible region is also provided. Overall, the document focuses on developing a flexible VAE representation that can accommodate multiple clustering constraints and prevent posterior collapse.

structure.title: Method: Historical Consensus Training
structure.summary:
The partial document describes a machine learning training process called Historical Consensus Training, which involves multiple stages to refine clustering results. The main points covered include: 
1. An overview of the Historical Consensus Training process, which consists of running Expectation-Maximization (EM) multiple times to obtain diverse clusterings, iteratively training a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with the current clusterings, and refining the results to achieve ultra-low loss.
2. A detailed description of the three stages of the training process: 
   - Stage 1: Power-of-Two Selection, where the VAE is trained with multiple clustering results and the best half are retained until only two clusterings remain.
   - Stage 2: Consensus Refinement, where the VAE is further trained with the final two clustering results until a high precision threshold is met.
   - Stage 3: Final Single-Cluster Training, where the VAE is trained with a single clustering result to verify that it does not collapse.
3. A theoretical analysis of the Historical Consensus Training process, including definitions of the Historical Loss and the Feasible Region, which provide a framework for understanding the training process and its convergence properties.

nodes.title: Motivation: Multiplicity as a Resource
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes a machine learning training process called Historical Consensus Training, which involves multiple stages to refine clustering results. The main points covered include: 
1. An overview of the Historical Consensus Training process, which consists of four stages: running EM multiple times, iteratively training a VAE, refining with the final two clusterings, and training with a single clustering.
2. Stage 1: Power-of-Two Selection, where the VAE is trained with multiple clustering results, and the best half are retained based on their performance.
3. Stage 2: Consensus Refinement, where the final two clustering results are used to continue training until a high precision threshold is met.
4. Stage 3: Final Single-Cluster Training, which serves as a stress test to detect any signs of collapse.
5. A theoretical analysis of the Historical Barrier, including definitions of Historical Loss and Feasible Region, which provide a framework for understanding the training process.

nodes.title: The Selection Pipeline
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses variational autoencoders (VAEs) and their training methods, focusing on preventing posterior collapse. It covers the basics of VAEs, including the evidence lower bound (ELBO) and the role of the encoder and decoder. The document also explores Gaussian mixture models (GMMs) and their application in conditioning VAE training. A new method called Historical Consensus Training is introduced, which leverages the multiplicity of clustering results from the EM algorithm to prevent posterior collapse. The method involves iteratively training the VAE with multiple clustering constraints and refining the model to satisfy these constraints. Theoretical analysis is provided, including definitions of historical loss and feasible region, to support the proposed method. Overall, the document presents a novel approach to training VAEs that promotes flexibility and prevents collapse by incorporating multiple clustering constraints.

nodes.title: Theoretical Analysis: The Historical Barrier
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes a machine learning training process called Historical Consensus Training, which involves multiple stages to refine clustering results. The main points covered include: 
1. An overview of the Historical Consensus Training process, which consists of four stages: running EM multiple times, iteratively training a VAE, refining with the final two clusterings, and training with a single clustering.
2. Stage 1: Power-of-Two Selection, where the VAE is trained with multiple clustering results and the best half are retained until only two remain.
3. Stage 2: Consensus Refinement, where the model is trained until it satisfies both clustering results to a high precision.
4. Stage 3: Final Single-Cluster Training, which serves as a stress test to detect any signs of collapse.
5. A theoretical analysis of the Historical Barrier, including definitions of Historical Loss and Feasible Region, which provide a framework for understanding the training process.

nodes.title: Why Power-of-Two?
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses a machine learning approach called Historical Consensus Training, which involves training a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) model using multiple clustering results. The main points covered include: 

1. An overview of the Historical Consensus Training process, which consists of four stages: running Expectation-Maximization (EM) multiple times, iteratively training the VAE with all current clusterings, refining with the final two clusterings, and training with a single clustering to verify non-collapse.

2. A detailed description of each stage, including the power-of-two selection stage, consensus refinement stage, and final single-cluster training stage.

3. Theoretical analysis of the Historical Consensus Training approach, including definitions of historical loss and feasible region, and proofs of lemmas and theorems related to the approach.

4. An explanation of the choice of power-of-two selection and its benefits, including creating a clear hierarchy of constraints and guaranteeing rapid convergence.

5. An introduction to experimental setup and datasets used to evaluate the Historical Consensus Training approach.

structure.title: Experiments
structure.summary:
The partial document discusses a historical consensus training algorithm for Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and its theoretical properties. The main points covered include: 
1. The historical consensus training algorithm, which involves iterative refinement of VAE parameters based on multiple clusterings of the data.
2. Theoretical results, such as the nesting of feasible regions and the exclusion of collapsed solutions, which provide insights into the algorithm's behavior.
3. The concept of "historical inertia," which explains how models refined on multiple constraints can retain a "memory" that prevents collapse even when constraints are removed.
4. The choice of power-of-two initial clusterings and halving each round, which creates a clear hierarchy of constraints and ensures rapid convergence.
5. The setup for experimental evaluation of the algorithm on various datasets.

nodes.title: Experimental Setup
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a historical consensus training algorithm for Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) that prevents collapse by retaining a "memory" of discarded clusterings. The algorithm involves multiple rounds of training with different clusterings, where the number of clusterings is halved at each round. The document also presents theoretical results, including lemmas and theorems, that provide insights into the algorithm's behavior and its ability to exclude collapsed solutions. The experimental section evaluates the algorithm on several datasets, including synthetic, MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10, and compares its performance with other VAE variants. The results show that the algorithm achieves high KL divergence values, indicating that it prevents collapse, but also highlight the need for future work to improve the distribution of information across latent dimensions. Additionally, the document verifies the existence of a "historical barrier" that prevents the model from collapsing even when trained with a single clustering.

nodes.title: Quantitative Results
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses experiments and results related to training Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) on various datasets, including Synthetic GMM, MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10. The main points covered include the description of the datasets, comparison with baseline methods such as Vanilla VAE and β-VAE, and the architecture used for the experiments. The document also presents evaluation metrics, including KL divergence, active units, reconstruction error, and Frechet Inception Distance. The results show that the proposed method achieves high KL values and prevents complete collapse, but tends to concentrate information into a small subset of dimensions. Additionally, the document verifies the retention of historical memory in the trained models and demonstrates separation from collapsed solutions.

nodes.title: Verification of Historical Barrier
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses experiments and results related to training Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) on various datasets, including Synthetic GMM, MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10. The main points covered include the description of the datasets, comparison with baseline methods such as Vanilla VAE and β-VAE, and the architecture used for the experiments. The document also presents evaluation metrics, including KL divergence, active units, reconstruction error, and Frechet Inception Distance. The results show that the proposed method achieves high KL values and prevents complete collapse, but tends to concentrate information into a small subset of dimensions. Additionally, the document verifies the retention of historical memory in the trained models and demonstrates separation from collapsed solutions.

nodes.title: Posterior Collapse Prevention
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a novel approach to preventing posterior collapse in variational autoencoders (VAEs) by leveraging the multiplicity of Gaussian mixture model (GMM) clusterings. The proposed method, called Historical Consensus Training, involves iteratively selecting and refining a set of candidate GMM priors through alternating optimization and selection. The key insight is that models trained to satisfy multiple distinct clustering constraints develop a "historical barrier" that prevents posterior collapse. The document provides theoretical analysis, experimental results, and comparisons to existing methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach in preventing posterior collapse and achieving non-collapsed representations. The main points covered include the introduction of Historical Consensus Training, the concept of historical barrier, and the experimental evaluation of the method on various datasets.

nodes.title: Ablation Studies
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the prevention of posterior collapse in variational autoencoders (VAEs). The main points covered include the comparison of the proposed method with baseline methods, such as Vanilla VAE, β-VAE, and KL Annealing, in terms of KL divergence and active units. The results show that the proposed method achieves the highest KL divergence and prevents posterior collapse across various datasets, including Synthetic, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and MNIST. The document also presents ablation studies on the initial number of clusterings, refinement threshold, and selection ratio, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method. Additionally, the method is shown to be architecture-independent, achieving non-collapsed representations with convolutional encoders/decoders. Overall, the proposed method is found to be effective in preventing posterior collapse and maintaining high KL divergence throughout training.

nodes.title: Architecture Independence
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the effectiveness of a method in preventing posterior collapse in variational autoencoders (VAEs). The main points covered include the method's ability to maintain high KL divergence throughout training, its performance on the CIFAR-10 dataset with convolutional architecture, and its architecture independence. The document also explores the effects of refinement threshold and selection ratio on the method's performance, and compares it to other methods, including the stability condition derived by Li et al. The results show that the method outperforms baselines and achieves non-collapsed representations, even under violating conditions.

nodes.title: Comparison with Stability Condition
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a novel framework called Historical Consensus Training, which prevents posterior collapse in Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) by leveraging the multiplicity of Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) clusterings. The method involves iteratively training the VAE with multiple clustering constraints, retaining the best-performing half of the candidates, and refining the training process until only two candidates remain. The document also provides theoretical analysis, experimental results, and comparisons with baseline methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of Historical Consensus Training in preventing posterior collapse and achieving non-collapsed representations. The main points covered include the introduction of Historical Consensus Training, its motivation, the selection pipeline, theoretical analysis, experimental setup, quantitative results, verification of historical barrier, and ablation studies.

structure.title: Discussion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the prevention of posterior collapse in variational autoencoders (VAEs). It presents experimental results comparing the proposed method with baseline methods, including Vanilla VAE, β-VAE, and KL Annealing. The results show that the proposed method achieves the highest KL divergence, indicating the least collapse, across all datasets. The document also presents ablation studies, including the effect of the initial number of clusterings on the final KL divergence. Additionally, it discusses the maintenance of historical memory and the prevention of posterior collapse through refinement and single-cluster training. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in preventing posterior collapse and maintaining a stable representation.

nodes.title: The Nature of Historical Memory
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a novel approach to preventing posterior collapse in variational autoencoders (VAEs) called Historical Consensus Training. The method leverages the multiplicity of Gaussian mixture model (GMM) clusterings to develop a historical barrier that prevents collapse. The main points covered include the introduction of Historical Consensus Training, its motivation, and the selection pipeline. The document also provides theoretical analysis, experimental results, and ablation studies to demonstrate the effectiveness of the method in preventing posterior collapse. Additionally, it discusses the nature of historical memory, limitations, and comparison with stability conditions. The key findings include the ability of the method to achieve non-collapsed representations, maintain high KL divergence, and prevent posterior collapse even under violating conditions.

nodes.title: Limitations
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the results and implications of a study on preventing posterior collapse in Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and its extension to diffusion models. The main points covered include the effect of initial number of clusterings and refinement threshold on the model's performance, the model's ability to encode historical memory, and the limitations of the method such as computational cost and choice of hyperparameters. The document also explores the broader implications of the study, including a new paradigm for preventing undesirable solutions in deep learning, and its potential application to other models such as Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and energy-based models. Additionally, the document discusses the analogy between posterior collapse in VAEs and information loss in diffusion models, and proposes a historical consensus training method for diffusion models to prevent mode dropping and unconditional generation degradation.

structure.title: Implications for Diffusion Models
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the main points of a research work focused on preventing undesirable solutions in deep learning, specifically posterior collapse in Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and its analogy in diffusion models. The key points covered include the choice of hyperparameters, clustering quality, and limited active units in VAEs. The document also explores the broader implications of the work, including a new paradigm for preventing undesirable solutions by leveraging solution multiplicity. Additionally, it discusses the implications for diffusion models, including the posterior collapse analogy, the spectral condition, and the adaptation of historical consensus training to diffusion models to prevent information loss and mode dropping.

nodes.title: Historical Consensus for Diffusion Training
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the implications of a new paradigm for preventing undesirable solutions in deep learning, specifically in the context of Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and diffusion models. The main points covered include the choice of optimal values for certain parameters, the quality of clustering, and the limitations of active units in VAEs. The document also explores the broader implications of this work, including the potential to extend the principle of leveraging solution multiplicity to other models with multiple valid solutions. Additionally, the document analyzes the posterior collapse in diffusion models, drawing an analogy with VAEs, and proposes a historical consensus training method to prevent information loss in the reverse process. The document also makes empirical predictions for diffusion models, provides preliminary evidence, and outlines future research directions, including adaptive scheduling, historical sampling, theoretical unification, and architecture design. Overall, the document presents a new perspective on generative modeling, suggesting that posterior collapse is a fundamental phenomenon that can be addressed through a unified theoretical framework.

nodes.title: Empirical Predictions
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Historical Consensus for Diffusion Models, a training algorithm that utilizes multiple noise schedules to improve the performance of diffusion models. The main points covered include the description of the algorithm, empirical predictions based on VAE analysis, preliminary evidence supporting these predictions, and future research directions. The predictions made include the existence of a critical timestep, the role of spectral condition, and the benefits of historical consensus in preventing schedule-independent collapse and improving inference flexibility. The document also highlights the potential for adaptive scheduling, historical sampling, theoretical unification, and architecture design to further improve diffusion models. Overall, the partial document provides a framework for understanding and improving diffusion models, suggesting that posterior collapse is a fundamental phenomenon in generative modeling.

The main points covered include the description of the algorithm, empirical predictions based on VAE analysis, preliminary evidence supporting these predictions, and future research directions. The predictions made include the existence of a critical timestep, the role of spectral condition, and the benefits of historical consensus in preventing schedule-independent collapse and improving inference flexibility. The document also highlights the potential for adaptive scheduling, historical sampling, theoretical unification, and architecture design to further improve diffusion models. Overall, the partial document provides a framework for understanding and improving diffusion models, suggesting that posterior collapse is a fundamental phenomenon in generative modeling.

nodes.title: Future Directions
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses Historical Consensus Training for diffusion models, a method that prevents posterior collapse by iteratively selecting among multiple noise schedules. The main points covered include the introduction of Algorithm 2 for Historical Consensus, empirical predictions based on VAE analysis, and preliminary evidence supporting these predictions. The document also explores future research directions, such as adaptive scheduling, historical sampling, and theoretical unification. Additionally, it touches on the concept of a critical timestep in diffusion models, beyond which the signal becomes indistinguishable from noise. The overall goal is to improve the understanding and performance of diffusion models, and to develop a unified theory for generative modeling.

structure.title: Conclusion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of posterior collapse in Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and introduces a novel method called Historical Consensus Training to prevent it. The document covers the critical timestep in diffusion models, the prevention of posterior collapse, and the empirical validation of the proposed method across multiple datasets and architectures. It also touches on the idea that while the method prevents collapse, the representation is not fully distributed, and future work will focus on encouraging more balanced use of latent dimensions. The document includes references to various research papers related to VAEs, diffusion models, and generative modeling.

nodes.title: Computational Resources
nodes.summary: The partial document covers the proof of Theorem 1, which discusses the properties of a collapsed solution in the context of clustering and dimensionality reduction. It also provides experimental details, including hyperparameters used in experiments on various datasets such as Synthetic, MNIST, Fashion, and CIFAR-10. Additionally, it mentions computational resources used and provides additional results, including the evolution of cluster retentions across rounds. The main points include the independence of the reconstruction from a specific variable, the derivation of a key equation, and the discussion of the implications of the theorem on the clustering results.

It also provides experimental details, including hyperparameters used in experiments on various datasets such as Synthetic, MNIST, Fashion, and CIFAR-10. The main points covered include the independence of the reconstruction from a specific variable, the relationship between clustering results and component means, and the experimental setup and results.

nodes.title: Evolution of Cluster Retentions
nodes.summary: The partial document covers the proof of Theorem 1, which discusses the properties of a collapsed solution in the context of clustering and variational autoencoders (VAEs). It also provides experimental details, including hyperparameters used in experiments on various datasets such as Synthetic, MNIST, Fashion, and CIFAR-10. Additionally, the document presents additional results, including the evolution of cluster retentions across rounds and sensitivity to the clustering loss weight β, with figures and tables supporting the findings. The main points covered include the theoretical proof, experimental setup, and analysis of results, focusing on the compatibility of clusterings with the VAE's representation and the impact of hyperparameters on performance.

nodes.title: Sensitivity to β
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the convergence of a selection process in a VAE's representation and the sensitivity of the model to the clustering loss weight β. It presents figures showing the retention pattern across rounds and the final KL divergence as a function of β, indicating that optimal performance occurs when β is in the range of 2.0 to 3.0.
